chunks.text: Stereo. HC JD A 38. Judgment Sheet IN THE LAHORE HIGH COURT, BAHAWALPUR BENCH, BAHAWALPUR. JUDICIAL DEPARTMENT Criminal Appeal No. 746-J of 2019 (Habib Ullah Vs. The State.) J U D G M E N T Date of hearing: 05.04.2022. Appellant by: Syed Aasim Ali Bukhari, Advocate. State by: Jam Muhammad Tariq, Deputy Prosecutor General. Complainant by: Ch. Muhammad Azhar, Advocate. SADIQ MAHMUD KHURRAM, J.– Habib Ullah son of Ali Ahmad (convict) was tried along with Sana Ullah and Inayat Ullah, the co- accused of the convict, both since acquitted, by the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Hasilpur in the case FIR No.212 of 2017 dated 13.06.2017 registered in respect of offences under sections 302 and 34 PPC at the Police Station Saddar Hasilpur, District Bahawalpur for committing the Qatl-i-Amd of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased). The learned trial court vide judgment dated 27.11.2019, convicted Habib Ullah son of Ali Ahmad (convict) and sentenced him as infra: Habib Ullah son of Ali Ahmad: Imprisonment for life under section 302(b) PPC as Tazir for committing Qatl-i-Amd of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) and directed to pay Rs.100,000/- as compensation under section 544-A, Cr.P.C. to the legal heirs of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) and in case of default thereof, the convict was further directed to undergo six months simple imprisonment. Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 2 The learned trial court, however, did not extend the benefit available under section 382-B of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 to the convict. Sana Ullah and Inayat Ullah, the co-accused of the convict, were both acquitted by the learned trial court. 2.

Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 2 The learned trial court, however, did not extend the benefit available under section 382-B of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 to the convict. Sana Ullah and Inayat Ullah, the co-accused of the convict, were both acquitted by the learned trial court. 2. Feeling aggrieved, Habib Ullah son of Ali Ahmad (convict) lodged the instant Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 through jail, assailing his conviction and sentence. 3. Precisely, the necessary facts of the prosecution case, as narrated by Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1), the complainant of the case are as under:- “Stated that marriage of my sister was solemnized with Habib-u-llah accused present in the court before 14/15 years. She was residing with him. Two days before the occurrence, my sister called me through cell phone and told me that Habib Ullah tortured at her and threatened her of dire-consequences. She asked me to come in her house. On 12.6.2017, I went at the house of my sister situated at Chak No.181/M. When we reached there, Habib Ullah was not present in the house. I alongwith pws Muhammad Ijaz S/O Ghulam Rasool, Babar Hussain S/O Muhammad Safdar stayed in the house of my sister situated in Chak No.181/M. We slept in front of Bathik. At about 1:00 A.M, at night we heard hue and cry from inside the house. I alongwith Pws Ijaz and Babar went inside the house. The light of bulb was on. In the light of bulb we saw that Habib Ullah armed with hatchet and Sana-u-llah armed with hatchet were standing there. On our seeing, accused Habib-u-llah inflicted hatchet blow, which landed at the head of my sister Parveen Bibi. My sister raised her hand to rescue herself, accused Sana-u-llah made hatchet blow, which landed at wrist of her left arm. Accused Habib-u-llah made another hatchet blow, which landed at left eye of my sister Parveen Bibi. I alongwith pws came forward to Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 3 rescue my sister, Habib-u-llah and Sana-u-llah threatened us not to come near, otherwise, they will also be killed.

Accused Habib-u-llah made another hatchet blow, which landed at left eye of my sister Parveen Bibi. I alongwith pws came forward to Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 3 rescue my sister, Habib-u-llah and Sana-u-llah threatened us not to come near, otherwise, they will also be killed. Accused persons while waiving their hatchets fled away from the place of occurrence. Blood was oozing from the head of my sister. I alongwith pws took my sister to Hospital. In hospital, police came there. Lady doctor referred my sister to BVH Bahawalpur in the accompany of police constable. My sister was admitted in BHV in Neurosurgical ward. Doctors started her treatment. On 13.06.2017, Haji Muhammad ASI reached at BVH Bahawalpur. I gave written, complaint Ex.P.A to Haji Muhammad ASI, which bears my signature in Urdu Ex.P.A/1. On 17.6.2017, Haji Muhammad ASI again came at BVH Bahawalpur to record statement of my sister. She was in Comma; she was not in condition to give statement. Constable handed over the clothes of my sister to Haji Muhammad ASI, Shalwar P.1, Qameez blood stained P.2 alongwith MLC. Haji Muhammad took into possession the above said articles vide recovery memo Ex-P.B, which was signed by me and I.O recorded my statement in this regard. On 18.6.2017, my sister died due to the injuries caused by Habib-u-1lah and Sana-u-11ah. I informed police of police station. Haji Muhammad reached at BVH Bahawalpur, he received dead body of my sister and came back alongwith him to THQ Hospital Hasilpur. Shamas-u-Din Sl alongwith other police officials were also present in the Hospital. Fozia Noreen lady constable was also present there. Shamas-ul-Din prepared injury statement. Dr. Fiaza conducted post mortem of my sister. After post mortem Shamas-ul-Din handed over the dead body to me through receipt Ex.P.C. After this, police alongwith dead body went with us. We left the dead body at our house and then moved forward at the place of occurrence alongwith police.

After this, police alongwith dead body went with us. We left the dead body at our house and then moved forward at the place of occurrence alongwith police. On our pointation, Shamas ul-Din prepared site plan and recorded our statements. Police came back to police station and we went back to our home for arranging funeral ceremony. On 6.7.2017, accused Habib-u-llah was arrested. On 12.07.2017, 1 alongwith pws. namely Babar and Ijaż came to police station to join investigation. 1.0 was inquiring from Habib-u-llah about the hatchet. Accused Habib-u-llah told that hatchet was present in his house and he can get recovered the same. Police Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 4 sat me alongwith Muhammad Ijaz and accused Habib-u llah in police Dallah and took us at the house of Habib-u llah situated in Chak No.181/M. Accused Habib-u-llah while moving forward got recovered blood stained hatchet P.3 lying towards eastern wall of eastern room lying behind trunk present on Patti. Habib-u-llah handed over blood stained hatchet to police. 1.0 took into possession hatchet the above said recovered blood stained hatchet P.3 vide recovery memo Ex.P.D and recorded our statements in this regard. The motive behind the occurrence was that there was a dispute of land between Habib-u- llah and Sana-u-llah with Shoukat Pannu. Accused Anait-u-llah advised them to prepare occurrence of murder and involve Shoukat Pannu in murder case of my sister. Accused present in the court be penalized.” 4. After the formal investigation of the case report under section 173 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 was submitted before the learned trial court wherein the accused were sent to face trial. The learned trial court framed the charge against the accused on 08.11.2017, to which the accused pleaded not guilty and claimed trial. 5.

After the formal investigation of the case report under section 173 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 was submitted before the learned trial court wherein the accused were sent to face trial. The learned trial court framed the charge against the accused on 08.11.2017, to which the accused pleaded not guilty and claimed trial. 5. The prosecution in order to prove its case got statements of as many as eleven witnesses recorded. The ocular account of the case was furnished by Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2). Muhammad Fayyaz, ASI (PW-3) stated that on 13.06.2017 he recorded the formal F.I.R (Exh.PA/2). Manzoor ul Haq 24/HC (PW-4) stated that on 18.06.2017 the Woman Medical Officer handed over the last worn clothes of the deceased to the Investigating Officer of the case, after the post mortem examination of the dead body and on 22.06.2017, Shahid Bashir, SI handed over ten photographs (P-11/1-10) of the dead body of the deceased to Shamas-ul- Din, SI (PW-10) who took the same into possession through recovery memo (Exh. PH). Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 5 Muhammad Sadiq 1987/HC (PW-6) stated that on 12.07.2017, Shamas-ul- Din, SI (PW-10), the Investigating Officer of the case, handed over to him one sealed parcel said to contain a hatchet which on 18.07.2017 he handed over the said sealed parcel to Amjad Iqbal, ASI (PW-5) for its onward transmission to the office of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore. Muhammad Shahid Imran, Draftsman (PW-7) prepared the scaled site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh. PJ). Haji Muhammad, ASI (PW-9) investigated the case on 13.06.2017 and detailed the facts of the investigation as conducted by him in his statement before the learned trial court. Shamas-ul- Din, SI (PW-10), the Investigating Officer of the case, investigated the case from 18.06.2017 till 16.08.2017, arrested the appellant on 06.07.2017, and detailed the facts of the investigation as conducted by him in his statement before the learned trial court. 6.

Haji Muhammad, ASI (PW-9) investigated the case on 13.06.2017 and detailed the facts of the investigation as conducted by him in his statement before the learned trial court. Shamas-ul- Din, SI (PW-10), the Investigating Officer of the case, investigated the case from 18.06.2017 till 16.08.2017, arrested the appellant on 06.07.2017, and detailed the facts of the investigation as conducted by him in his statement before the learned trial court. 6. The prosecution also got Dr. Faiza Saeed (PW-8) examined who on 13.06.2017, was posted as Woman Medical Officer at the THQ hospital, Hasilpur and on the same day had medically examined Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (then injured later deceased). On examining Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (then injured later deceased), Dr. Faiza Saeed (PW-8) had observed as under:- “Description of Injuries: 1. A lacerated wound of size about 5 x 4 cm x bone exposed situated on left frontal region of skull, swelling present on it KUO 2. A lacerated wound of size about 1 x I cm x skin deep present on the posterior lateral aspect of right forearm KUO. Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 6 3. A lacerated wound of size about 2 x 1 cm x skin deep present on the end of left eye brow black eye bruise present around the left eye along with swelling KUO Then I referred the patient to BV. Hospital, Bahawalpur for further management and treatment. MLC NO.FS 62/2017 EXh.P.L. is the correct computerized copy of the MLC of the patient and signed and stamped by me.” Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar died on 18.06.2017 and on the same day Dr. Faiza Saeed (PW-8) conducted the post mortem examination of the dead body of the deceased namely Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar and noted the same injuries on her dead body as she had observed when she had medically examined her. Dr. Faiza Saeed (PW-8) further opined with regard to her opinion as to the cause of death of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) as under:- “ After thorough external and internal examination of the dead body. I am of the opinion that above described injuries are ante-mortem in nature and caused by some blunt weapon Injury NO.1 is main cause of death which caused fracture to left temporo parietal region of skull which is confirmed by report received from BVH.

Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) as under:- “ After thorough external and internal examination of the dead body. I am of the opinion that above described injuries are ante-mortem in nature and caused by some blunt weapon Injury NO.1 is main cause of death which caused fracture to left temporo parietal region of skull which is confirmed by report received from BVH. Bahawalpur detail is given on page No.1. which caused injury to vital organ (brain) leading to coma and death. Such types of injury are usually sufficient to cause death in ordinary course of nature.” The prosecution also got Dr. Amin Ullah Khan (PW-11) examined who on 13.06.2017 was posted as Medical Officer at BV hospital Bahawalpur and had examined Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (then injured later deceased). On examining Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (then injured later deceased), Dr. Amin Ullah Khan (PW-11) had observed as under:- Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 7 “On C.T scan brain there is multiple fracture of left temporo parietal bone of skull and brain contusion. At time of receiving pupils were dilated and non-responding to light, operation was done, operative findings are skull fractures and multiple dural tear and brain contusion evacuated and dural repair done with graft. After that patient was shifted to Neuro ICU for post op-care, where she could not improve and expired on 18.06.2017 due to above said injuries. Copy of Surgical/treatment notes (MLC) Ex P.R bearing my signatures in English is prepared and signed by me.” 7. On 24.09.2020, the learned Assistant District Public Prosecutor gave up the prosecution witness namely Fouzia Noreen 15/LC as being unnecessary. On 11.02.2019, the learned Assistant District Public Prosecutor closed the prosecution evidence after tendering in evidence the report of Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore regarding the analysis of the blood stained earth (Exh.PS) and the report of Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore regarding the analysis of the blood stained hatchet (Exh.PT). 8.

On 24.09.2020, the learned Assistant District Public Prosecutor gave up the prosecution witness namely Fouzia Noreen 15/LC as being unnecessary. On 11.02.2019, the learned Assistant District Public Prosecutor closed the prosecution evidence after tendering in evidence the report of Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore regarding the analysis of the blood stained earth (Exh.PS) and the report of Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore regarding the analysis of the blood stained hatchet (Exh.PT). 8. After the closure of prosecution evidence, the learned trial court examined the appellant namely Habib Ullah son of Ali Ahmad under section 342 Cr.P.C. and in answer to the question why this case against you and why the PWs have deposed against you, he replied that he had been involved in the case falsely and was innocent. The appellant namely Habib Ullah son of Ali Ahmad opted not to get himself examined under section 340 Cr.P.C. and did not adduce any evidence in his defence. 9. At the conclusion of the trial, the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Hasilpur convicted and sentenced the appellant as referred to above. Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 8 10. The contention of the learned counsel for the appellant precisely is that the whole case is fabricated and false and the prosecution remained unable to prove the facts in issue and did not produce any unimpeachable, admissible, and relevant evidence. Learned counsel for the appellant further contended that the story of the prosecution mentioned in the statements of the witnesses, on the face of it, was highly improbable. Learned counsel for the appellant further contended that the statements of the prosecution witnesses were not worthy of any reliance. The learned counsel for the appellant also submitted that the recovery of the hatchet (P-3) was full of procedural defects, of no legal worth and value, and result of fake proceedings. The learned counsel for the appellant also argued that the appellant had been involved in the occurrence due to enmity. The learned counsel for the appellant finally submitted that the prosecution has totally failed to prove the case against the appellant beyond the shadow of a doubt. 11.

The learned counsel for the appellant also argued that the appellant had been involved in the occurrence due to enmity. The learned counsel for the appellant finally submitted that the prosecution has totally failed to prove the case against the appellant beyond the shadow of a doubt. 11. On the other hand, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General alongwith the learned counsel for the complainant contended that the prosecution has proved its case beyond the shadow of a doubt by producing independent witnesses. The learned Deputy Prosecutor General alongwith the learned counsel for the complainant further argued that the deceased died as a result of injuries suffered at the hands of the appellant. The learned Deputy Prosecutor General alongwith the learned counsel for the complainant further contended that the medical evidence also corroborated the statements of the prosecution witnesses namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2). The learned Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant further argued that the recovery of the hatchet (P-3) from the appellant also corroborated the ocular account. The learned Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 9 Deputy Prosecutor General along with the learned counsel for the complainant contended that there was no occasion for the prosecution witnesses, who were related to the deceased, to substitute the real offender with the innocent in this case. Lastly, they prayed for the rejection of the appeal. 12. I have heard the learned counsel for the appellant, the learned counsel for the complainant, the learned Deputy Prosecutor General and with their able assistance, perused the record and evidence recorded during the trial. 13. The whole prosecution case revolves around the statements of Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2), the eye witnesses of the occurrence. The relationship of the said witnesses with the deceased is on record.

The whole prosecution case revolves around the statements of Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2), the eye witnesses of the occurrence. The relationship of the said witnesses with the deceased is on record. Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) was the real sister of Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1). It was also admitted by the prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) that the prosecution witness namely Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) was closely related to him and admitted during cross-examination as under:- “This Court has relationship witness with pw Ijaz and his family since our forefathers. My grand-father had relationship with grandfather of Ijaz PW.” The prosecution witnesses namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) were also admittedly not the residents of the place of occurrence. According to the prosecution witnesses namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2), both of them were residents of Chak No.163-M, whereas the occurrence had taken place at Chak No.181-M, which was admittedly at a distance from the place of residences of Pervaiz Akhtar (PW- 1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2). The prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) during cross-examination stated as under:- Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 10 “ The distance between Chak No. 163/M and Chak No. 181/M is 2/3 kilometers.” (emphasis supplied) The prosecution witness namely Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) during cross- examination stated as under:- “The distance between Chak No. 163/M to Chak No. 181/M is 2/ 2 1⁄2 kilometers.” (emphasis supplied) The above referred portion of the cross-examination of Pervaiz Akhtar (PW- 1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) reveals that it was an admitted fact that both the prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) did not have their residences or their places of employment near or around the place of occurrence.

According to the prosecution witnesses namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2), both of them were residents of Chak No.163-M, whereas the occurrence had taken place at Chak No.181-M, which was admittedly at a distance from the place of residences of Pervaiz Akhtar (PW- 1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2). The prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) during cross-examination stated as under:- Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 10 “ The distance between Chak No. 163/M and Chak No. 181/M is 2/3 kilometers.” (emphasis supplied) The prosecution witness namely Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) during cross- examination stated as under:- “The distance between Chak No. 163/M to Chak No. 181/M is 2/ 2 1⁄2 kilometers.” (emphasis supplied) The above referred portion of the cross-examination of Pervaiz Akhtar (PW- 1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) reveals that it was an admitted fact that both the prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) did not have their residences or their places of employment near or around the place of occurrence. In this manner, both the prosecution witnesses namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) can be validly termed as “chance witnesses” and, therefore, were under a bounden duty to provide a convincing reason for their presence at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence and were also under a duty to prove their presence by producing some physical proof of the same. This Court has noted with grave concern that the prosecution witnesses namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) failed miserably to provide any consistent evidence as to the reason for their arrival at the place of occurrence and their presence at the place of occurrence, when the same was taking place. In order to prove their presence at the place of occurrence at the time of occurrence, Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) stated that the deceased namely Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) called him about two days before the occurrence and asked him to come to her house and after receiving the said call of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased), Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) travelled to the place of occurrence, reaching there on 12.06.2017. Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) failed to prove that he had gone to the place of occurrence after receiving the call of Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 11 Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) and admitted during cross-examination that he did not proceed to the house of the deceased after receiving her call but only went to her house on 12.06.2017 i.e., after two days. The prosecution witness, namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1), also admitted that he even did not send his brother, namely Babar Hussain to the house of Mst.

Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) and admitted during cross-examination that he did not proceed to the house of the deceased after receiving her call but only went to her house on 12.06.2017 i.e., after two days. The prosecution witness, namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1), also admitted that he even did not send his brother, namely Babar Hussain to the house of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) on the day when he had received her call. Prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) admitted during cross-examination as under:- “When my sister telephonically called me, at that day, I did not go into the house of my sister. It is correct that next date of receiving telephonic call; I did not go in the house of my sister................... When I received telephonic all of my sister regarding altercation at that day, Babar Hussain PW was present in the house. When I received telephonic all (sic) of my sister regarding altercation, I did not ask Babar Hussain PW to go in the house of our sister Parveen Bibi. My brother Babar also did not go in the house of our sister on the day of receiving of call and next day of receiving of call.” (emphasis supplied) Moreover, this Court has noted that the prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) made a failed attempt to provide a reason for his not going to the matrimonial house of the Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) after receiving her call and stated that as he was in the city of Lahore when he had received the call, therefore, he could not proceed immediately to the house of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased). The prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) admitted during cross-examination as under:- “Volunteered, I was in Lahore. When I received telephonic call regarding altercation of my sister, I was in Lahore.” The above referred stance of the prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) that he did not go to the house of Mst.

The prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) admitted during cross-examination as under:- “Volunteered, I was in Lahore. When I received telephonic call regarding altercation of my sister, I was in Lahore.” The above referred stance of the prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) that he did not go to the house of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 12 Muhammad Safdar (deceased) after receiving her call because he was at Lahore, was also proved to be a false statement as during the cross- examination, Haji Muhammad, ASI (PW-9), the Investigating Officer of the case, stated that the prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) never told him that when he received the call of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased), he was at Lahore. Haji Muhammad ASI (PW- 9), the Investigating Officer of the case during cross-examination, stated as under:- “No witness recorded in his statement u/s 161 Cr PC that complainant was at Lahore when he received a cell phone call of his sister prior to the occurrence.” Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) during the investigation of the case and even before the learned trial court also did not produce any evidence in the shape of Call Data Record (CDR) of the mobile phone number under his use or under the use of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) to establish that he had indeed received the call of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) two days before the occurrence and after receiving the said call, he and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) had proceeded to the place of occurrence. Furthermore, Shamas-ul-Din, SI (PW-10), the Investigating Officer of the case, as well as Haji Muhammad, ASI (PW-9), the first Investigating Officer of the case, also did not collect any evidence during the investigation of the case so as to prove that Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) had received a phone call of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased), two days before the occurrence asking him to come to her house.

Furthermore, Shamas-ul-Din, SI (PW-10), the Investigating Officer of the case, as well as Haji Muhammad, ASI (PW-9), the first Investigating Officer of the case, also did not collect any evidence during the investigation of the case so as to prove that Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) had received a phone call of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased), two days before the occurrence asking him to come to her house. The mobile phone device through which the deceased had called Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) was also not found present at the place of occurrence nor was produced by any witness during the investigation of the case. The Woman Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 13 Medical Officer, who medically examined Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) on 13.06.2017 and who conducted the post mortem examination of the dead body of the deceased, did not find any such mobile phone device. The Investigating Officers of the case, also did not find any such mobile phone device through which Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased), had called the prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1), two days before the occurrence, at the place of occurrence, despite the fact that both Shamas-ul-Din, SI (PW-10), the Investigating Officer of the case, and Haji Muhammad, ASI (PW-9) remained present at the place of occurrence for a continued period and there was no possibility that the said mobile phone device could have been removed from the place of occurrence. The failure of the prosecution to produce the mobile phone device under the use of Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) has repercussions resulting in the conclusion that the very inception of the prosecution case is put in doubt due to the said failure of the prosecution. In this manner, both the eye witnesses namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) failed miserably to establish the reason for their presence at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence. 14. Another fact, which denudes the falsity of the claim of the prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) that Mst.

In this manner, both the eye witnesses namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and Muhammad Ejaz (PW-2) failed miserably to establish the reason for their presence at the place of occurrence, at the time of occurrence. 14. Another fact, which denudes the falsity of the claim of the prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) that Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) had called him two days before the occurrence, is that Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) had contracted marriage without the consent of her parents and her relatives including Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) and, therefore, there were no visiting terms between Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) and the prosecution witness namely Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1). Shamas-ul- Din, SI (PW- Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 14 10), the Investigating Officer of the case, during cross-examination admitted that it was Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) himself who told him that he had never visited the place of occurrence prior to the day of occurrence. Shamas-ul-Din, SI (PW-10), the Investigating Officer of the case, during cross-examination stated as under:- “ The pws got recorded before me that marriage of Habibullah and Parveen Bibi was result of love affair. In my investigation complainant never disclosed before me that he ever visited the house of Habibullah at any occasion. In my investigation, there is nothing on record about the fact that the complainant has visiting terms with his sister Parveen Bibi deceased or not.” More importantly, Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) himself admitted that he did not have any usual visiting terms with Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased). Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) during cross-examination stated as under:- “ Nikah of my sister with accused Habibullah remained intact for 14/15 years. At the time of murder, age of my sister was about 40 years. At the time of marriage with Habib, my sister was divorced lady.

At the time of murder, age of my sister was about 40 years. At the time of marriage with Habib, my sister was divorced lady. We have limited visiting terms with our sister..” (emphasis supplied) In this scenario, when according to Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1) himself, he had limited visiting terms with the Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased), then there did not exist any possibility that Mst. Parveen Bibi daughter of Muhammad Safdar (deceased) would have called Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1), who, in the fifteen years of marriage, had never ever earlier called and complained about the appellant to Pervaiz Akhtar (PW-1). This narrative of the prosecution witnesses that the accused kept waiting for the arrival of the witnesses at the place of occurrence and thereafter committed the same, in their presence and in their view, in the dead of the night, is unnatural and cannot be believed. It is opposed to human conduct that an assailant would proceed with the commission of the crime despite the arrival of the witnesses prior to the commission of the offence. It is all the more illogical that being perceptive of the fact that the accused ran the risk of the witnesses deposing against the accused, even then the assailant committed the occurrence in their presence when he already knew that the witnesses were present at the place of occurrence.

The question for determination before this Court now is whether the evidence of the prosecution witnesses which has been disbelieved qua the acquitted co- accused of the appellant can be believed against the appellant. The proposition of law in Criminal Administration of Justice, that a common set of witnesses can be used for recording acquittal and conviction against the accused persons who were charged for the commission of the same offence, is now a settled proposition. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan has held that partial truth cannot be allowed and perjury is a serious crime. This view stems from the notion that once a witness is found to have lied about a material aspect of a case, it cannot then be safely assumed that the said witness will declare the truth about any other aspect of the case. This Court has noted that the view should be that "the testimony of one detected in a lie was wholly worthless and must of necessity be rejected." If a witness is not coming out with the whole truth, then his evidence is liable to be discarded as a whole, meaning thereby that his evidence cannot be used either for convicting accused or acquitting some of them facing trial in the same case. This proposition is enshrined in Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 36 the maxim falsus in uno falsus in omnibus. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in Criminal Miscellaneous Application No. 200 of 2019 in Criminal Appeal No. 238-L of 2013 reported as PLD 2019 Supreme Court 527 has enunciated the following binding principles: - “The Pakistan Penal Code, 1860 (P.P.C.) contains many offences dealing with perjury and giving false testimony.

This proposition is enshrined in Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 36 the maxim falsus in uno falsus in omnibus. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in Criminal Miscellaneous Application No. 200 of 2019 in Criminal Appeal No. 238-L of 2013 reported as PLD 2019 Supreme Court 527 has enunciated the following binding principles: - “The Pakistan Penal Code, 1860 (P.P.C.) contains many offences dealing with perjury and giving false testimony. The very fact that there is a whole chapter, numbered XI, dedicated to such offences amply testifies to the fact that matters relating to giving of testimony were taken very seriously by those who drafted the P.P.C. and their continued retention in the P.P.C. ever since reflects the will of the legislature, which is the chosen representative body of the people of Pakistan through which they exercise their authority within the limits prescribed by Almighty Allah. The following sections, listed under Chapter XI titled "Of False Evidence And Offences Against Public Justice", highlight the fact that giving false testimony has been treated to be a very serious matter entailing some serious punishments................................................. Holding that the rule falsus in uno, falsus in omnibus is inapplicable in this country practically encourages commission of perjury which is a serious offence in this country. A court of law cannot permit something which the law expressly forbids............................................................................................. 21. We may observe in the end that a judicial system which permits deliberate falsehood is doomed to fail and a society which tolerates it is destined to self-destruct. Truth is the foundation of justice and justice is the core and bedrock of a civilized society and, thus, any compromise on truth amounts to a compromise on a society's future as a just, fair and civilized society. Our judicial system has suffered a lot as a consequence of the above mentioned permissible deviation from the truth and it is about time that such a colossal wrong may be rectified in all earnestness.

Therefore, in light of the discussion made above, we declare that the rule falsus in uno, falsus in omnibus shall henceforth be an integral part of our jurisprudence in criminal cases and the same shall be given effect to, followed and applied by all the courts in the country in its letter and spirit. It is also directed that a witness found by a court to have resorted to a deliberate falsehood on a material aspect shall, without Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 37 any latitude, invariably be proceeded against for committing perjury.” Guided by the said judgment of the august Supreme Court of Pakistan, This Court has examined the prosecution evidence. It has been argued by the learned Deputy Prosecutor General and the learned counsel for the complainant that where any person dies an unnatural death in the house of such accused then some part of the onus lies on him to establish the circumstances in which such unnatural death had occurred. The prosecution is bound to prove its case against an accused person beyond a reasonable doubt at all stages of a criminal case and in a case where the prosecution asserts the presence of some eye-witnesses and such claim of the prosecution is not established by it, there the accused person could not be convicted merely on the basis of a presumption that since the murder of a person had taken place in his house, therefore, it must be he and none else who would have committed that murder. The learned Deputy Prosecutor General submits that it was in the knowledge of the appellant how the deceased died so it was the appellant who was responsible, in absence of any explanation. The law on the burden of proof, as provided in Article 117 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984, mandates the prosecution to prove, and that too, beyond any doubt, the guilt of the accused for the commission of the crime for which he is charged. The said provision provides: “117. Burden of proof:- Whoever desires any Court to give judgment as to any legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts which he asserts, must prove that those facts exist. When a person is bound to prove the existence of any fact, it is said that the burden of proof lies on that person.” On a conceptual plain, Article 117 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984 enshrines the foundational principle of our criminal justice system, whereby the accused is presumed to be innocent unless proved otherwise.

Burden of proof:- Whoever desires any Court to give judgment as to any legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts which he asserts, must prove that those facts exist. When a person is bound to prove the existence of any fact, it is said that the burden of proof lies on that person.” On a conceptual plain, Article 117 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984 enshrines the foundational principle of our criminal justice system, whereby the accused is presumed to be innocent unless proved otherwise. Accordingly, the burden is placed on the prosecution to prove beyond doubt the guilt of the accused which burden can never be shifted to the accused, unless the legislature by express terms commands otherwise. It is only when the prosecution is able to Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 41 discharge the burden of proof by establishing the elements of the offence, which are sufficient to bring home the guilt of the accused then, the burden is shifted upon the accused, inter alia, under Article 122 of the Qanun-e- Shahadat, 1984, to produce evidence of facts, which are especially in his exclusive knowledge, and practically impossible for the prosecution to prove, to avoid conviction. Article 122 reads as under: “122. Burden of proving fact especially within knowledge:- When any fact is especially within the knowledge of any person, the burden to proving that fact is upon him.” It has to be kept in mind that Article 122 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984 comes into play only when the prosecution has proved the guilt of the accused by producing sufficient evidence, except the facts referred in Article 122 Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984, leading to the inescapable conclusion that the offence was committed by the accused. Then, the burden is on the accused not to prove his innocence, but only to produce evidence enough to create doubts in the prosecution's case. It may be noted that this issue was also dilated upon by the august Supreme Court of Pakistan in the case of “Rehmat alias Rahman alias Waryam alias Badshah v. The State” (PLD 1977 SC 515), where, while deliberating upon Section 106 of the Evidence Act, which is para materia with Article 122 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984, it held as under: “Needless to emphasis that in spite of section 106 of the Evidence Act in criminal case the onus rests on the prosecution to prove the guilt of the accused beyond reasonable doubt and this section cannot be construed to mean that the onus at any stage shifts on to the accused to prove his innocence or make up for the inability and failure of the prosecution to produce evidence to establish the guilt of the accused.

It may be noted that this issue was also dilated upon by the august Supreme Court of Pakistan in the case of “Rehmat alias Rahman alias Waryam alias Badshah v. The State” (PLD 1977 SC 515), where, while deliberating upon Section 106 of the Evidence Act, which is para materia with Article 122 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984, it held as under: “Needless to emphasis that in spite of section 106 of the Evidence Act in criminal case the onus rests on the prosecution to prove the guilt of the accused beyond reasonable doubt and this section cannot be construed to mean that the onus at any stage shifts on to the accused to prove his innocence or make up for the inability and failure of the prosecution to produce evidence to establish the guilt of the accused. Nor does it relieve! the prosecution of the burden to bring the guilt home to the accused. It is only after the prosecution has on the evidence adduced by it, succeeded in raising reasonable inference of the guilt of the accused, unless the same is rebutted, that this section wherever applicable, comes into play and the accused may negative the inference by proof of some facts within his special knowledge. If, however, the Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 42 prosecution fails to prove the essential ingredients of the offence, no duty is cast on the accused to prove his innocence." The ratio decidendi of the above decision was further developed by in the case of “NASRULLAH alias NASRO Versus The STATE (2017 S C M R 724), wherein, it held as under: “It has been argued by the learned counsel for the complainant that in the cases of Arshad Mehmood v. The State (2005 SCMR 1524) and Saeed Ahmed v. The State (2015 SCMR 710) this Court had held that where a wife of a person or any vulnerable dependent dies an unnatural death in the house of such person then some part of the onus lies on him to establish the circumstances in which such unnatural death had occurred. The learned counsel for the complainant has maintained that the stand taken by the appellant regarding suicide having been committed by the deceased was neither established by him nor did it fit into the circumstances of the case, particularly when the medical evidence contradicted the same. Be that as it may holding by this Court that some part of the onus lies on the accused person in such a case does not mean that the entire burden of proof shifts to the accused person in a case of this nature.

The learned counsel for the complainant has maintained that the stand taken by the appellant regarding suicide having been committed by the deceased was neither established by him nor did it fit into the circumstances of the case, particularly when the medical evidence contradicted the same. Be that as it may holding by this Court that some part of the onus lies on the accused person in such a case does not mean that the entire burden of proof shifts to the accused person in a case of this nature. It has already been clarified by this Court in the case of Abdul Majeed v. The State (2011 SCMR 941) that the prosecution is bound to prove its case against an accused person beyond reasonable doubt at all stages of a criminal case and in a case where the prosecution asserts presence of some eye-witnesses and such claim of the prosecution is not established by it there the accused person could not be convicted merely on the basis of a presumption that since the murder of his wife had taken place in his house, therefore, it must be he and none else who would have committed that murder.................................................................. In a case of this nature the appellant could not have been convicted for the alleged murder merely because he happened to be the husband of the deceased.” In a criminal case, the burden of proof is on the prosecution and article 122 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984 is certainly not intended to relieve it of that duty. On the contrary, it is designed to meet certain exceptional cases in which it would be impossible, or at any rate disproportionately difficult, for the prosecution to establish facts which are " especially " within the knowledge of the accused and which he could prove without difficulty or inconvenience. If the article was to be interpreted otherwise, it would lead to the very startling conclusion that in a murder case the burden lies on the accused to prove that he did not commit the murder because who could know better than he whether he did or did not. Article 122 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984 cannot be used Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 43 to undermine the well-established rule of law that, save in a very exceptional class of cases, the burden is on the prosecution and never shifts.

Throughout the web of the Law, one golden thread is always to be seen, that it is the duty of the prosecution to prove the accused's guilt subject to any statutory exception. No matter what the charge, the principle that the prosecution must prove the guilt of the accused is the law and no attempt to whittle it down can be entertained. Benjamin Franklin, who was one of the leading figures of early American history, went further arguing "it is better a hundred guilty persons should escape than one innocent person should suffer." All the contradictions noted by the learned High Court are sufficient to cast a shadow of doubt on the prosecution's case, which entitles the petitioner to the right of benefit of the doubt. It is a well settled principle of law that for the accused to be afforded this right of the benefit of the doubt it is not necessary that there should be many circumstances creating uncertainty and if there is only one doubt, the benefit of the same must got to the petitioner. This Court in the case of Mst. Asia Bibi v. The State (PLD 2019 SC 64) while relying on the the earlier judgments of this Court has categorically held that "if a single circumstance creates reasonable doubt in a prudent mind about the apprehension of guilt of an accused, then he/she shall be entitled to such benefit not as a matter of grace and concession, but as of right. Reference in this regard may be made to the cases of Tariq Pervaiz v. The State (1998 SCMR 1345) and Ayub Masih v. The State (PLD 2002 SC 1048)." The same view was reiterated in Abdul Jabbar v.

The State (1998 SCMR 1345) and Ayub Masih v. The State (PLD 2002 SC 1048)." The same view was reiterated in Abdul Jabbar v. State (2010 SCMR 129) when this court observed that once a single loophole is observed in a case presented by the prosecution, such as conflict in the ocular account and medical evidence or presence of eye-witnesses being doubtful, the benefit of such loophole/lacuna in the prosecution's case automatically goes in favour of an accused.” 29. For what has been discussed above, Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 lodged by Habib Ullah son of Ali Ahmad (appellant), is allowed and the conviction and sentence of the appellant awarded by the learned trial court through the impugned judgment dated 27.11.2019 are hereby set-aside. The appellant namely Habib Ullah son of Ali Ahmad is ordered to be acquitted by extending him the benefit of the doubt. The appellant namely Habib Ullah son Criminal Appeal No.746-J of 2019 52 of Ali Ahmad is in custody and is directed to be released forthwith if not required in any other case.
